warnings: LiPo batteries can catch fire or explode if overcharged, over-discharged, punctured, or short-circuited.
Always use a dedicated LiPo charger with balancing — never charge with a generic charger.
Never leave charging LiPo batteries unattended.
Store in a fireproof LiPo bag at 3.7–3.85V per cell (storage voltage).
Inspect for swelling before each use — discard any puffy pack immediately.
Never discharge below 3.0V per cell; use a low-voltage cutoff (LVC) circuit.
Keep away from flammable materials and extreme heat.
Li-Ion cells require a protection circuit module (PCM/BMS) to prevent overcharge and over-discharge.
Never short-circuit Li-Ion cells — thermal runaway risk.
Use only chargers rated for Li-Ion chemistry at the correct voltage.
Inspect for damage, dents, or leaking electrolyte before use.
Lithium-based batteries are flammable and require proper charging and handling.
Use the correct charger for your specific lithium chemistry (LiPo, Li-Ion, LiFePO4).
Never puncture, crush, or expose to water.
Laser diodes >5mW can cause permanent eye damage in milliseconds — never look into the beam.
Always wear appropriate laser safety goggles rated for the wavelength and power level.
Post hazard signs and enclose the beam path where possible.
Class 3B/4 lasers require a key switch and emission indicator.
Be aware of reflective surfaces that can redirect the beam unexpectedly.
MAINS VOLTAGE (120V/240V AC) IS LETHAL — a single contact can cause fatal electrocution.
ALL mains wiring must be performed by a qualified electrician and comply with local electrical codes.
Use properly rated enclosures, breakers, and insulated terminals rated for mains voltage.
Double-insulate or earth-ground all metal enclosures.
Install a ground-fault circuit interrupter (GFCI/RCD) for any mains-connected project.
Verify isolation from mains before touching any component.
AC mains wiring is lethal. Do not work on live circuits.
Use properly rated components and follow local electrical codes.
Always isolate and verify dead before touching conductors.
High-voltage circuits are potentially lethal even when powered off due to stored charge in capacitors.
Discharge capacitors with a bleeder resistor before handling.
Maintain safe creepage and clearance distances on PCB layouts.
Use appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).
UV/SLA resin is a skin and eye irritant — wear nitrile gloves and safety glasses.
Work in a well-ventilated area; resin fumes can cause respiratory irritation.
Uncured resin is hazardous waste — cure all waste resin before disposal.
Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) used for washing is flammable — keep away from heat sources.
Wash hands thoroughly after handling even with gloves.
FDM printer hotends reach 200–280°C — do not touch during or immediately after printing.
Some filaments (ABS, ASA) emit fumes that may be irritating — print in a ventilated area or enclosure with filtration.
Keep flammable materials away from heated bed and hotend.
ABS filament emits styrene fumes during printing — use adequate ventilation or an enclosure with activated carbon filter.
Hotend temperatures required (230–250°C) can cause burns on contact.
MOSFETs switching inductive loads (motors, solenoids) require a flyback diode to prevent voltage spikes that can destroy the device.
Ensure gate drive voltage is appropriate for the selected MOSFET (logic-level vs standard).
Add a gate resistor (10–100Ω) to reduce EMI and ringing.
Motors are inductive loads — always use a flyback diode or motor driver with built-in protection.
Mechanical moving parts create pinch and entanglement hazards — add guards where appropriate.
Motor stall current can be 5–10× running current — size fuses and drivers accordingly.
Servo motors can exert significant torque — keep fingers clear of linkages during testing.
Ensure servo power supply can handle peak stall current without browning out the MCU.
Electrolytic capacitors are polarized — reverse connection causes venting or rupture.
Large capacitors store significant charge — discharge before handling in powered-down circuits.
Check voltage rating — always use capacitors rated at least 20% above circuit voltage.
Relays switching AC or high DC voltages require safe isolation between coil and contact sides.
Use a flyback diode across the relay coil to protect the driving transistor/MCU.
Ensure contact current rating exceeds the load by at least 20%.
Soldering iron tips reach 300–450°C — risk of burns and fire.
Solder flux fumes are respiratory irritants — solder in a ventilated area or use a fume extractor.
Lead solder requires hand washing after handling; consider lead-free alternatives.
Even low-voltage batteries can deliver high short-circuit currents capable of causing burns or fires.
Fuse battery outputs appropriately for the circuit.
Dispose of batteries according to local regulations — do not dispose of in general waste.
18650 Li-Ion cells without integrated protection can deliver >20A short-circuit current.
Use cells with built-in protection circuit or add external BMS/protection board.
Never short-circuit bare 18650 cells — fire and explosion risk.
Verify cell polarity before insertion into holders.
High-frequency PWM signals on long wires can cause EMI — use decoupling capacitors near loads.
Ensure PWM frequency does not interfere with I2C or other communication buses on shared grounds.
High-current traces and wires generate heat — size conductors appropriately for the load.
Use terminal blocks or XT connectors rated for the current; do not use pin headers for >2A.
Add a fuse or resettable polyfuse at the power input.
Hot glue gun tip and nozzle reach ~200°C — risk of burns.
Hot glue is not rated for high-temperature environments — do not use near motors or power components that generate significant heat.
